metrics.narrative: Kyle Campbell articulated clear operational pain on the 26 Mar 2026 discovery call: Bronte's year-old procurement function runs on Smartsheet (an improvised ERP, 'Excel on steroids'), driving 'an extreme amount of administrative work' that procurement wants to eliminate. The team uses eight separate software tools including Jonas for budgeting, and Kyle expressed the goal to 'segregate that by going to one software' to consolidate. Construction projects face daily scope changes and require local union-compliant vendor sourcing, which the current manual Smartsheet workflow cannot handle efficiently. However, NO quantified financial or time-cost impact was stated (no cycle-time baseline, no FTE hours on manual RFI/invoice entry, no annual admin cost or error rate). Kyle's interest was strong ('does seem like something that could benefit us big time'), but the business case remains qualitative. The gap: quantify the admin burden (hours/week on manual invoice OCR, RFI vendor research, contract redlining) and translate consolidation from eight tools to one into a defensible ROI (cost avoidance, time saved, error reduction, faster project close). Without a quantified value case, an Economic Buyer or CFO approval is hard to secure. Make building that business case—co-developed with Kyle, anchored to real project data—a ranked move.

competition.narrative: Procore is the sole named competitor and the primary strategic threat, status ACTIVE, threat level HIGH. On the 26 Mar 2026 discovery call Kyle stated Bronte is 'looking into' Procore as an all-in-one construction project management + accounting + light procurement platform. Kyle acknowledged 'Procore's procurement stuff might be way more in depth than theirs' (implied: less deep than Zycus) but emphasized Procore's appeal is consolidation: 'it's convenient in the sense it has the accounting, project management, and procurement in one… it would be nice to know if Zycus has that capability.' When Bailey confirmed Zycus does NOT do project accounting ('Procore is best in business for construction… Zycus is strictly procurement'), Kyle responded that adding Zycus as a ninth tool competes with Bronte's goal to go from eight tools to one, but he remained open if Zycus integrates deeply with Procore via API ('curious if they'd be able to do a plug-in… Procore has so many extensions and partnerships'). The competitive wedge is DEPTH vs. BREADTH: Zycus owns deeper, AI-driven Source-to-Pay procurement (union vendor auto-discovery, ML-based compliance exclusion, autonomous negotiation agents, contract-clause risk scanning, OCR invoice automation) that Procore's light procurement cannot match; Procore owns construction-native project accounting, budgeting (Jonas replacement), and all-in-one simplicity that Zycus cannot deliver alone. The deal hinges on whether Bronte prioritizes procurement transformation (Zycus wins) or construction-platform consolidation (Procore wins), and whether a Zycus-Procore API integration is feasible and low-friction enough that Kyle can sell 'best of both' to ownership rather than forcing a choice. Bailey noted on the call that Procore has open APIs and Zycus can integrate, but admitted '70% sure we don't do project accounting' and 'I don't know if we've done a Procore integration before.' That uncertainty is a vulnerability: if Zycus cannot prove a clean, reference-backed Procore integration, Kyle defaults to Procore's good-enough procurement to avoid adding a tenth tool and a custom integration project. The 64-day silence may mean Kyle is deep in Procore eval and Zycus is on hold. How we win: (1) prove the Procore integration is real—deliver a reference customer (construction/infrastructure vertical) running Zycus procurement + Procore project management via API, with integration timeline and cost transparency; (2) quantify the procurement-depth ROI that Procore cannot deliver (time saved on manual union vendor research, invoice OCR eliminating manual AP entry, autonomous RFI negotiation cutting 10-15% off vendor pricing, contract-risk AI reducing legal review cycles); (3) position Zycus + Procore integration as the 'best of breed' path vs. Procore-only as the 'good enough but manual procurement' path, anchored to Kyle's stated pain (extreme admin burden, tool sprawl, budget control); (4) get Kyle and a Procore-integration SE in front of ownership together for a joint 30-min exec briefing that shows the integration is turnkey, not a science project. If Zycus cannot prove Procore integration feasibility with a reference and a timeline, this deal is at high risk of losing to Procore.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed, specific, and owned. Kyle Campbell (Manager, Procurement) is the pain owner. On the 26 Mar 2026 discovery call Kyle articulated four operational and financial pains Bronte's year-old procurement function faces daily. First: extreme manual administrative burden. Bronte runs procurement on Smartsheet ('Excel on steroids'), which Kyle described as 'not designed for procurement, we just make it work,' causing 'extreme amount of administrative work' that procurement wants to eliminate. He uses eight separate software tools (Smartsheet for workflow, Jonas for project budgeting, others unnamed) to complete the job, and stated the goal is 'trying to segregate that by going to one software.' Invoice processing is manual entry into the ERP; RFI vendor sourcing is manual research; contract authoring and redlining is manual. Second: construction-specific sourcing complexity. Projects for City of Toronto, TTC, Metrolinx require local, union-compliant vendors. 'Everything's changing every day. We don't know what we're doing on a daily basis' due to daily scope changes. Kyle described the manual pain: 'when I do an RFI now, I have to manually add vendors… based on my knowledge I know which are union. If the system's picking up every masonry contractor in Toronto and half aren't union, I'm wasting their time and my time.' Non-union vendors create contract-violation risk ('we can get into trouble'). Third: lack of spend control and budget overruns. Before procurement existed a year ago, 'it was the wild west—guys pulling trigger on money left, right, and center' with no oversight, leading to 'uncontrolled expenditures' and busted budgets. Kyle brought procurement in 'to maintain control of spend,' but the Smartsheet workflow lacks approval gates, so project managers can still bypass procurement if not tightly policed. Fourth: tool sprawl blocking consolidation. The eight-tool stack creates data silos, prevents unified spend visibility, and complicates executive reporting. Kyle is evaluating Procore for construction project management + light procurement vs. best-of-breed procurement (Zycus) integrated via the relevant stakeholder pain is real, urgent (daily), and expensive (admin FTE time, budget overruns, compliance risk), but NOT quantified—no cycle-time baseline, no annual cost of manual work, no error rate or savings target. The pain is confirmed qualitatively; the next step is to quantify it with Kyle so ownership sees a defensible ROI.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process read from the call sequence: Kyle Campbell (Manager, Procurement) is the operational evaluator and internal champion-builder, but explicitly NOT the decision-maker. On 26 Mar he stated he must 'present it to ownership' with demos and pricing before any commitment. The approval chain runs: Kyle evaluates and builds business case → Kyle presents to 'ownership' (undefined: likely CEO/CFO/COO given $120k SaaS spend) → ownership decides. No multi-stakeholder buying committee visible; construction procurement in a mid-market firm often runs lean (procurement + finance + exec), consistent with Kyle being the sole engaged contact. The process Kyle is running: (1) discovery call 26 Mar to understand Zycus fit; (2) review Showpad demo videos and ballpark pricing offline; (3) schedule April follow-up to see full requisition-to-pay workflow demo; (4) present findings to ownership with visual demos; (5) ownership decision (timeline unstated). However, the process stalled after step 1. Kyle requested the deliverables for step 2 on 26 Mar; two follow-ups were scheduled (23 Apr, 7 May) but neither converted (not recorded, cancelled). As of 26 Jun, 64 days later, there is no evidence Kyle received or reviewed the materials, no evidence of step 3 (the deeper demo), and no movement toward step 4 (the ownership presentation). The process gap: no explicit timeline, no forcing function, no confirmation of what ownership needs to see to greenlight next stage (a full POC? reference calls? a written proposal with ROI?), and no clarity on whether Bronte has a formal vendor-selection process (RFP, competitive eval, procurement committee) or if this is an informal sole-source eval. The 64-day silence may mean Kyle is stuck (too busy with fiscal year-end and inventory, as he noted, or unable to get ownership's attention), OR the process has quietly shifted to evaluating Procore exclusively and Zycus is deprioritized. Without direct contact with ownership or a forcing event (contract expiration, project launch, budget approval deadline), the process is indefinite. Next move: re-engage Kyle, confirm he received the Showpad videos and pricing, ask what blockers are preventing the April follow-up from rescheduling, and propose a joint 30-minute executive briefing with his ownership contact where Zycus and Kyle co-present a one-page business case (pain, solution fit, ballpark ROI, Procore integration path) to get ownership engaged and create a decision timeline.

decision_criteria.narrative: Kyle surfaced four explicit evaluation criteria on the 26 Mar 2026 discovery call, each tied to Bronte's construction-procurement pain. First: vendor sourcing by geography and union compliance—the system must auto-discover local union contractors for City of Toronto, TTC, Metrolinx projects and exclude non-union vendors to avoid contract violations ('if the system's picking up every masonry contractor in Toronto and half of them aren't union, I'm wasting their time and my time'). Kyle confirmed interest in AI-driven vendor questionnaires that pre-screen union status and machine-learning that remembers past exclusions. Second: project-level spend tracking and accounting integration. Kyle uses Jonas software to track budgets and spend per job and asked whether Zycus offers that capability or integrates with project-management platforms like Procore, which Bronte is evaluating. He noted, 'right now our company uses 8 different softwares… we're trying to segregate that by going to one software'—consolidation is a priority, but adding a ninth tool (Zycus) competes with Procore's all-in-one appeal unless Zycus integrates deeply. Third: requisition-to-PO workflow control. Kyle emphasized procurement must review and approve every requisition before esourcing to avoid the 'wild west' of uncontrolled project-manager spending ('guys pulling trigger on money left, right, and center… that's how we bust budgets'). He needs approval gates tied to dollar thresholds and buyer roles, NOT an Amazon-style self-checkout that bypasses procurement. Fourth: contract/spec intelligence—Kyle built a ChatGPT assistant that reads contract specs and answers 'I need 1000 bricks, what are the size/install requirements per the TTC spec?'; he asked if Zycus can ingest contract documents and auto-populate RFQ line-item specs from them. These four criteria are confirmed and specific. However, they are Kyle's operational lens, not a formal scorecard weighted by ownership or a multi-stakeholder RFP rubric. The Decision Criteria gap: no visibility into what 'ownership' (the EB/CEO/CFO) weighs most heavily—TCO vs. Procore integration feasibility, speed-to-value, construction-vertical depth, or SaaS vs. on-prem. And no confirmation whether these four must-haves are deal-breakers or negotiable if Zycus cannot fully deliver (e.g. if project accounting must stay in Jonas/Procore and Zycus is procurement-only). Next move: after re-engaging Kyle, co-build a one-page decision matrix with him that maps his four criteria plus ownership's commercial/strategic priorities (budget, timeline, integration cost, change-management risk) and score Zycus vs. Procore on each, so the business case Kyle presents is structured and defensible.

Kyle's stated goal is to consolidate from eight tools to one, which Procore enables and Zycus alone does not. However, Kyle acknowledged Zycus's procurement is 'way more in depth' and expressed interest in a Procore-Zycus API integration ('curious if they can do a plug-in'). The deal hinges on proving Zycus-Procore integration is feasible, reference-backed, and turnkey—without that proof, Kyle defaults to Procore's good-enough procurement to avoid tool sprawl.
